flashcards.Back: A decentralized wireless model where each AP is standalone and self-contained, managing its own security policies and RF parameters without a central controller.
A centralized wireless model where 'dumb' APs rely on a Wireless LAN Controller (WLC) for management, security, and control functions.
The division of labor in lightweight AP systems where real-time 802.11 functions stay on the AP, while non-real-time management functions move to the WLC.
Control and Provisioning of Wireless Access Points. An IETF standard tunneling protocol used for communication between lightweight APs and a WLC.
UDP port 5246, used for management and configuration traffic between the WLC and its associated lightweight access points.
UDP port 5247, used to transport encapsulated client data frames between the access point and the Wireless LAN Controller.
A wireless model where AP management is handled by a remote cloud platform (like Cisco Meraki) while the APs function as autonomous units for traffic forwarding.
A wireless deployment where the WLC software function is integrated directly into the hardware of a network switch (e.g., Catalyst 9300).
A wireless solution where the WLC function is hosted on an access point, allowing it to manage up to 100 other APs without a dedicated hardware controller.
The default operating mode for a lightweight AP. it provides BSSs to clients and scans for interference/rogue devices on other channels when not transmitting.
An AP mode designed for branch offices. it allows the AP to switch traffic locally even if the CAPWAP connection to the central WLC is lost.
A dedicated sensor mode where the AP does not transmit. it is used exclusively for IDS, rogue detection, and location services.
A diagnostic mode where the AP radios are dedicated to capturing all 802.11 frames on a channel and forwarding them to a remote analysis tool (like Wireshark).
A specialized AP mode that correlates MAC addresses between the wired and wireless networks to identify unauthorized rogue access points.
An AP mode used to create point-to-point or point-to-multipoint wireless links between buildings or to support wireless mesh networking.
A mode that dedicates the AP radios to spectrum analysis, discovering non-Wi-Fi sources of interference like microwaves or cordless phones.
Datagram Transport Layer Security. The protocol used to provide encryption for CAPWAP control messages between an AP and its WLC.
The central management device in a lightweight architecture that handles client authentication, roaming, security, and radio resource management.
A centralized design where the WLC is typically located in a core data center and manages APs across the entire enterprise network.
The basic building block of a WLAN, consisting of a single access point and its associated client stations.
An automated WLC feature that dynamically adjusts the power levels and channel assignments of all managed APs to optimize network performance.
In a centralized WLC model, this refers to the path user traffic takes from the AP, through the CAPWAP tunnel, to the WLC before entering the wired network.
The management traffic exchanged between the AP and WLC used for provisioning, monitoring, and maintaining the wireless infrastructure.
A component of RRM where the WLC automatically chooses the best operating channel for an AP to minimize interference with its neighbors.
A component of RRM where the WLC adjusts an AP's radio power to ensure adequate coverage while minimizing overlap between adjacent cells.
The process where a mobile client moves from the coverage area of one AP to another within the same ESS without losing network connectivity.
Service Set Identifier. A human-readable text string that identifies a specific wireless network, allowing users to select the correct signal for connection.
A security feature where APs in monitor mode or local mode scan for malicious wireless activity, such as deauthentication attacks or spoofed SSIDs.
The logical association where a WLC maps a specific SSID to a wired VLAN ID, ensuring wireless traffic enters the correct network segment.
Another term for a centralized AP that requires a WLC. it is 'lightweight' because it lacks the full management and security capabilities of an autonomous unit.
